review_summary: The transcript is sufficient for concept questions on implicit density modeling and the role of the latent variable in a basic GAN setup.

question_bank.question: Which statement best describes an implicit density model?
choices: It may not output a numeric p(x), but it can still generate samples from the learned distribution
It always provides an exact closed-form likelihood for every sample
It can only be used for conditional generation and not unconditional generation
It requires labels y at training time in order to sample
question_bank.explanation: Implicit density models focus on sampling rather than directly returning a tractable density value. They can still learn a useful data distribution even when p(x) is not explicitly available.

question_bank.question: In the basic GAN setup, what is the role of the latent variable z?
choices: It is sampled from a simple prior and mapped by the generator into candidate data samples
It stores the discriminator's binary real-or-fake label for each image
It is the exact log-likelihood of the training example under the data distribution
It is the gradient of the discriminator with respect to the model weights
question_bank.explanation: GANs begin from an easy-to-sample prior such as a Gaussian. The generator transforms z into outputs whose distribution should become similar to the real data distribution.
